scenario: Worker activates a job; gets jobKey=12345. Team wonders if job keys are unique + their lifecycle.
question: Job key uniqueness + lifecycle?

options.text: Job keys in Camunda 8 are not randomly generated values. They are deterministically assigned by the Zeebe broker at job creation time, following a monotonically increasing 64-bit integer pattern that ensures global uniqueness within the cluster. Random key generation would risk collisions and make ordering and traceability unreliable. The broker-assigned key provides a stable, globally unique identifier for each job activation, used by workers to reference the job in complete, fail, throw-error, and update-retries API calls throughout the job lifecycle.

Workers do not assign or choose job keys; they are generated by the Zeebe broker when the job is created. This ensures uniqueness and consistency regardless of which worker instance picks up the job. Workers receive the broker-assigned key as part of the activated job payload and use that same key to complete or fail the job. Allowing users to assign keys would risk collisions across concurrent workers and make the system's uniqueness guarantees impossible to maintain without external coordination.

Job keys are unique within the cluster: they are broker-generated 64-bit integers assigned at job creation time, guaranteed to be unique across all partitions. Workers receive the key when they activate a job and use it to call complete, fail, throwError, or updateRetries commands. Job keys are distinct from process instance keys and element instance keys -- each has its own keyspace and lifecycle. Keys are monotonically increasing per partition, enabling approximate ordering. Workers should log the job key for per-execution traceability and distributed debugging.

Job keys in Camunda 8 are guaranteed to be unique within the cluster. The Zeebe broker assigns keys from a monotonically increasing sequence per partition, ensuring no two jobs share the same key. This uniqueness is essential: workers use job keys to identify exactly which job to complete or fail, and uniqueness prevents accidental double-completion or incorrect targeting. The uniqueness guarantee holds even in multi-partition, high-throughput deployments.

a.text: Incorrect. Job key semantics: Generation: By broker at job creation. Unique within cluster: no two jobs share key. 64-bit integer typical. Monotonic / increasing: rough; partition-influenced. Use cases: Worker API calls: `java // Complete a job client.newCompleteCommand(jobKey) .variables(result) .send() .join(); // Fail a job client.newFailCommand(jobKey) .retries(0) .errorMessage("...") .send() .join(); // Throw BPMN error client.newThrowErrorCommand(jobKey) .errorCode("INVALID") .send() .join(); Job key identifies which job to act on. Lifecycle: Creation: Process reaches activity that needs a job (Service Task, etc.). Engine creates job with new key. Job ACTIVATABLE. Activation: Worker activates: job key returned to worker. Job state: ACTIVATED. Completion: Worker calls complete with key. Job removed; process advances. Re-activation (after timeout -- per Set 13 Q10): Same job key may be re-activatable (verify). Or new key on re-activation (per implementation). For tracking: Log job keys: per-execution traceability. Correlate: with process instance, element instance. Operate: searchable by job key. Multiple keys per process: Process instance key: identifies process instance. Element instance key: per element activation. Job key: per job activation. All different; all serve different purposes. For audit: Job keys for action tracking. "Worker X completed job Y at time Z." For idempotency: Worker can use job key for deduplication. "Don't repeat work for this job key." For SDK abstractions: Camunda Spring Boot Starter @JobWorker (replaces legacy spring-zeebe-sdk per camunda-spring-boot-starter/getting-started.md:16-22): auto-passes job key in ActivatedJob argument. Worker code typically doesn't see it directly (autoComplete). Manual completion: java @JobWorker(type = "process", autoComplete = false) public void handle(ActivatedJob job, JobClient client) { // do work client.newCompleteCommand(job.getKey()).send().join(); } ` Worker uses job key. In gRPC API: Activate jobs returns list with keys. Subsequent calls reference key. In REST API: Similar; job keys in URLs / bodies. For long-running workers: Worker stores job key while processing. On completion: uses key. If activation expires: new worker may get new key for same job. For monitoring: Per-job-key metrics: latency, retry count. Job-level traces: distributed tracing per job. Best practices: Log job keys: for traceability. Use in correlation: with external systems. Don't expose to end users: internal IDs. Anti-patterns: Using job key as business ID: wrong; use business variables. Hardcoding job keys: meaningless. For the scenario (jobKey=12345): Unique: no other job has key 12345. Lifecycle: from activation to completion. Use: pass to complete / fail / error APIs.
